perturbation.original_text: Grantor hereby grants to Grantee and Grantee hereby accepts a franchise to construct, reconstruct, operate, maintain, repair, and extend a Gas Distribution System within Grantor's Territorial Limits in accordance with the terms and conditions set forth below ("Franchise"). The Franchise is granted pursuant to Grantor's Franchise Ordinance (Ordinance No. 967), adopted November 17, 2015, ("Ordinance"), which is incorporated by reference herein, including any applicable definitions.
perturbation.changed_text: Grantor hereby grants to Grantee and Grantee hereby accepts a permit to construct, reconstruct, operate, maintain, repair, and extend a Gas Distribution System within Grantor's Territorial Limits in accordance with the terms and conditions set forth below ("Franchise"). The Franchise is granted pursuant to Grantor's Franchise Ordinance (Ordinance No. 967), adopted November 17, 2015, ("Ordinance"), which is incorporated by reference herein, including any applicable definitions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'franchise' to 'permit' creates a contradiction. While the document refers to a "Franchise Ordinance," the language now implies a permit system, confusing the legal basis for the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia State Law regarding franchise agreements
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia law distinguishes between franchises and permits. This section grants a 'franchise' but uses language suggesting a 'permit,' potentially invalidating the agreement under state law if the correct procedures for granting a franchise were not followed.

perturbation.justification: The original contract grants a 'franchise' under the 'Franchise Ordinance,' while the changed text uses 'permit.' The Perturbation Explanation states this creates a contradiction because the document still refers to the 'Franchise Ordinance,' even though the language implies a permit system, which confuses the legal basis of the agreement. The Law Explanation supports this by stating Virginia law distinguishes between franchises and permits and that using 'permit' language may invalidate the agreement if franchise procedures weren't followed. The provided snippet, § 15.2-2108, refers to franchises in Title 15.2 (Counties, Cities and Towns), Chapter 21 (Franchises; Sale and Lease of Certain Municipal Public Property; Public Utilities). Although repealed, it suggests a legal framework differentiating franchises from other agreements like permits, thus supporting the contradiction created by misaligning the terminology. The change from 'franchise' to 'permit' introduces a potentially significant misalignment with legal requirements for franchises, making the contract unenforceable under the Franchise Ordinance, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined under 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: Grantee shall during the term of this Franchise maintain at least one business office within the Territorial Limits of Grantor. Such office shall be open at least forty (40) hours per week for the conduct of business between Grantee and its customers.
perturbation.changed_text: Grantee shall during the term of this Franchise maintain at least one mailing address within the Territorial Limits of Grantor. Such address shall be available for mail receipt only.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'business office' to 'mailing address' and restricting its function drastically reduces the accessibility and service availability to customers, potentially violating implied obligations of a franchise agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: By significantly reducing the physical presence and accessibility of the gas company, this modification could be construed as an unfair or deceptive trade practice under the Virginia Consumer Protection Act, as it misrepresents the level of service available to consumers within the franchise territory.

(Effective July 1, 2025) Prohibited practices LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 59.1. Virginia Consumer Protection Act 6/15/2025 § 59.1-200 . (Effective until July 1, 2025) Prohibited practices. A. The following fraudulent acts or practices committed by a supplier in connection with a consumer transaction are hereby declared unlawful: 1. Misrepresenting goods or services as those of another; 2. Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services; 3. Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association of the supplier, or of the goods or services, with another; 4. Misrepresenting geographic origin in connection with goods or services; 5. Misrepresenting that goods or services have certain quantities, characteristics, ingredients, uses, or benefits; 6. Misrepresenting that goods or services are of a particular standard, quality, grade, style, or model; 7. Advertising or offering for sale goods that are used, secondhand, repossessed, defective, blemished, deteriorated, or reconditioned, or that are "seconds," irregulars, imperfects, or "not first class," without clearly and unequivocally indicating in the advertisement or offer for sale that the goods are used, secondhand, repossessed, defective, blemished, deteriorated, reconditioned,
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a 'business office' open for 40 hours a week, implying a certain level of service and accessibility. The changed text replaces this with a 'mailing address' for mail receipt only. This significantly reduces the physical presence and accessibility, potentially misrepresenting the level of service available. The Law Explanation suggests this could be an unfair or deceptive trade practice under the Virginia Consumer Protection Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 59.1-200, lists prohibited practices, including '[m]isrepresenting that goods or services have certain quantities, characteristics, ingredients, uses, or benefits.' By reducing the accessibility and functionality of the physical location, the modified contract potentially misrepresents the characteristics and benefits of the service, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the original intent and potentially violates consumer protection laws by diminishing service accessibility.

perturbation.original_text: Grantee shall not discriminate on the basis of race, religion, color, sex, national origin, age, disability, or any other basis prohibited by state law relating to discrimination in employment, except where there is a bona fide occupational qualification reasonably necessary to the normal operation of the Grantee.
perturbation.changed_text: Grantee shall not discriminate on the basis of race, religion, color, or sex, except where there is a business necessity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'national origin, age, disability, or any other basis prohibited by state law relating to discrimination in employment' and changing 'bona fide occupational qualification' to 'business necessity' narrows the protected classes and weakens the standard for permissible discrimination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification violates federal anti-discrimination laws, specifically Title VII and the ADA, which prohibit discrimination based on national origin, age, and disability. Replacing the 'bona fide occupational qualification' standard with 'business necessity' also lowers the legal threshold for justifying discriminatory practices.

L. 88-352), as amended. Title VII is codified at 42 U.S.C. 2000e and in subsequent sections. Title VII amendments include those introduced by the Civil Rights Act of 1991 (CRA) and the Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act of 2009 . Editor’s notes appear in italics and include cross-references to the codified version of Title VII. Title VII prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex and national origin. An Act To enforce the constitutional right to vote, to confer jurisdiction upon the district courts of the United States to provide injunctive relief against discrimination in public accommodations, to authorize the attorney General to institute suits to protect constitutional rights in public facilities and public education, to extend the Commission on Civil Rights, to prevent discrimination in federally assisted programs, to establish a Commission on Equal Employment Opportunity, and for other purposes. Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, That this Act may be cited as the "Civil Rights Act of 1964". * * * DEFINITIONS SEC. 2000e. [Section 701] For the purposes of this subchapter- (a) The term "person" includes one or more individuals, governments, governmental agencies, political subdivisions, labor unions, partnerships, associations, corporations, legal representatives, mutual companies, joint-­stock companies, trusts, unincorporated organizations, trustees, trustees in cases under Title 11 [originally, bankruptcy ], or receivers. (b) The term "employer" means a person engaged in an industry affecting commerce who has fifteen or more employees for each working day in each of twenty or more calendar weeks in the current or preceding calendar year, and any agent of such a person, but such term does not include (1) the United States, a corporation wholly owned by the Government of the United States, an Indian tribe, or any department or agency of the District of Columbia subject by statute to procedures of the competitive s
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the scope of protected classes by removing 'national origin, age, disability, or any other basis prohibited by state law relating to discrimination in employment' and replaces 'bona fide occupational qualification' with 'business necessity.' This contradicts Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which prohibits employment discrimination based on 'race, color, religion, sex and national origin.' By removing national origin and other protected classes, the changed text creates a lower standard, weakening the legal protections against discrimination, creating a clear non-compliance.
